questions.question: According to Kurt Lewin's field theory, what determines behavior in group contexts?

options.text: Only the group environment and norms
Behavior is a function of both person AND environment (B = f(P,E))
Random chance with no predictable patterns

questions.explanation: Lewin's field theory states that Behavior = function of (Person, Environment), meaning group context powerfully shapes individual actions beyond personality alone. Groups develop their own culture, norms, hierarchy, and dynamics that can't be reduced to individual psychology. When navigating groups, observe system-level patterns (who speaks, who's silenced, what topics are taboo) beyond individual personalities to understand the true dynamics at play.

questions.question: According to Google's Project Aristotle research, what is the #1 predictor of high-performing teams?

options.text: Having the most talented individual members
Psychological safety—belief you can speak up, make mistakes, and disagree without punishment
Having the highest budget and resources

questions.explanation: Google's Project Aristotle found psychological safety is THE #1 predictor of high-performing teams—more than talent or resources. When people feel safe to voice concerns, admit mistakes, ask questions, and challenge ideas without fear of humiliation or rejection, teams make better decisions, learn faster, innovate more, and have higher engagement. Amy Edmondson's framework shows this requires framing work as learning, acknowledging fallibility, asking questions, responding constructively, and sanctioning disrespect.

questions.question: In Asch's conformity studies, what percentage of people conformed to the group's obviously wrong answer?
questions.explanation: Asch's studies showed 40% of people conformed to the group's obviously wrong answer, demonstrating powerful conformity pressure that can make individuals deny observable reality to fit in. This illustrates why encouraging dissent, assigning devil's advocates, and individual accountability are crucial counter-strategies. Groups naturally suppress dissent for harmony (groupthink), but this leads to poor decisions. Understanding conformity pressure helps you resist it and create space for diverse perspectives.

questions.question: According to Tuckman's stages of group development, what is the correct order of stages?
questions.explanation: Tuckman's stages proceed as Forming (polite, testing boundaries) → Storming (conflict emerges as members compete for roles) → Norming (cohesion develops, norms established) → Performing (peak productivity and satisfaction). Understanding these stages helps set appropriate expectations: storming is normal and necessary, not failure, and many groups get stuck there without working through conflict. If your group is in storming, engage in healthy conflict rather than avoiding it or fleeing.

questions.question: If a group you're part of punishes dissent, demands you cut ties with friends/family outside the group, uses shame and humiliation as control, and makes leaving feel impossible, what does this indicate?

options.text: This is normal group cohesion and you should stay committed
This is a sign you need to participate more to earn acceptance
These are red flags of an unhealthy/cult-like group requiring immediate exit—healthy communities respect autonomy and outside relationships
This means you're not a good fit for group membership

questions.explanation: These are serious red flags of unhealthy/cult-like control, not healthy community. Healthy communities respect individual autonomy and outside relationships, welcome questioning and diverse perspectives, have permeable boundaries (you can leave without punishment), and serve members' wellbeing. Groups demanding exclusivity, punishing dissent, leader worship, or exploitation (financial, emotional, sexual) are using control tactics, not building belonging. If a community violates consent or boundaries, your wellbeing matters more than group belonging—leave and seek support from people outside the group.

title: Group Dynamics & Navigating Social Settings — Understanding Social Identity Theory - Quiz
